narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 49.9% chance that you would not pass—high enough to trigger rejection as a protective measure for both you and the program. This prediction drew heavily from your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, your LSAT score of 35, and your Year 1 grade decile of 4, which together painted a picture of elevated risk compared to patterns seen in students who succeed. Among the top influences, your LSAT score stood out as the strongest positive factor, nudging the model toward believing you could pass since it showed solid potential despite not reaching the average of 37.27 seen in those who make it through the bar. In contrast, your Year 1 decile pushed firmly in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a higher chance of failure because it fell below the typical 6.04 decile for successful students, suggesting early academic hurdles that often persist. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely as another key concern, contributing toward the failure prediction as it lagged behind the 3.24 average of bar passers, reinforcing worries about sustaining the rigor needed for law school and beyond. These three elements overshadowed other aspects like your middle family income bracket or full-time study plans, which align reasonably with accepted students. Your Year 3 decile of 4 raised similar flags, though less dominantly, hinting at consistent rather than improving performance. Compared to typical admitted students—who often enter with stronger early grades, higher GPAs, and LSATs in the upper range—your profile appeared riskier, even as your background fits common patterns among passers. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive; it's the model highlighting where historical data shows the biggest barriers to bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your undergrad record through post-bac courses or relevant experience, and seeking targeted academic coaching to climb those grade deciles. Many students in similar spots have rebounded by addressing these core areas head-on, and reapplying with improvements could shift the odds dramatically in your favor. You're capable of this—focus on those actionable steps, and the data will start telling a different story.
